### Community standards for Nova Roma Horizon Innovation Society

**Nova Roma Horizon Innovation Society** (“**Nova Roma**”) hosts gatherings, programs, online spaces, and collaborations across our ecosystems. This Code of conduct (“**Code**”) sets expectations for everyone who participates—whether in person in Ontario or elsewhere, or through our websites, chats, video calls, and related channels.

We are committed to an environment that respects the dignity and equality of all people, consistent with the **Human Rights Code** of Ontario and applicable Canadian human rights law.

### Scope

This Code applies to all participants in Nova Roma activities, including attendees, speakers, sponsors, volunteers, staff, contractors, and guests. It applies at events, in official Nova Roma online forums, and in communications carried out in Nova Roma’s name or as part of its programs.

### Our commitment

We strive to provide a welcoming, respectful, and harassment-free experience for everyone, regardless of age, ancestry, colour, race, citizenship, ethnic origin, place of origin, creed, disability, family status, marital status, gender identity, gender expression, sex, sexual orientation, record of offences, receipt of public assistance, or any other ground protected by applicable human rights law.

### Expected behaviour

- Treat others with respect and professionalism.
- Use inclusive language and make space for different perspectives and experiences.
- Give and accept constructive feedback graciously.
- Respect boundaries, consent, and confidentiality when others share personal or sensitive information in community settings.
- Follow applicable laws and venue rules, including those of the Province of Ontario and Canada when activities take place here.

### Unacceptable behaviour

The following are not tolerated:

- Harassment, bullying, intimidation, stalking, or discrimination.
- Verbal abuse, slurs, or hostile comments related to any protected ground or personal characteristic.
- Unwelcome sexual attention, sexual comments, or unwelcome physical contact.
- Sustained disruption of sessions, talks, or shared online spaces.
- Retaliation against someone for reporting a concern or participating in an investigation.

### Reporting

If you experience or witness behaviour that violates this Code, you may:

- Contact us at [hello@novaromahorizon.org](mailto:hello@novaromahorizon.org), or  
- Use our [contact form](/forms/contact), or  
- Speak with on-site staff or organizers at in-person events when available.

Reports will be reviewed promptly and handled with attention to privacy and fairness, consistent with our [Privacy notice](/infopages/privacy). We may need to share information with those who need to know in order to address the report.

### Enforcement

Participants asked to stop unacceptable behaviour are expected to comply immediately. Nova Roma may take any action it reasonably deems appropriate, including warnings, removal from a session or channel, exclusion from future events or programs, or termination of sponsorship or volunteer relationships. Serious matters may be referred to law enforcement where appropriate.

Nothing in this Code limits your right to seek advice from human rights bodies or other authorities under Ontario or Canadian law.

### Acknowledgements

This Code builds on widely used community standards for inclusive participation. Nova Roma adapts them to our mission, our Ontario-based governance, and the expectations of our partners and participants.

Community standards for Nova Roma Horizon Innovation Society

### Privacy notice for Nova Roma Horizon Innovation Society (Ontario, Canada)

**Nova Roma Horizon Innovation Society** (“**Nova Roma**”, “**we**”, “**us**”, or “**our**”) respects your privacy. This notice describes how we collect, use, disclose, and safeguard personal information in connection with our websites, events, programs, newsletters, and related services (collectively, the “**Services**”), in accordance with Canada’s **Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act** (**PIPEDA**) and, where applicable, substantially similar provincial legislation and other Canadian privacy requirements.

This notice applies to personal information we collect in the course of operating the Services from individuals in Canada and elsewhere, unless a separate privacy statement applies (for example, certain employment or grant contexts).

### Who we are

Nova Roma Horizon Innovation Society is a Canadian non-profit organization. For privacy inquiries, contact us at [hello@novaromahorizon.org](mailto:hello@novaromahorizon.org) or through our [contact form](/forms/contact). You may also write to us at the mailing address we publish on this site when available.

### What personal information we collect

Depending on how you interact with us, we may collect:

- **Contact and identity information** — such as name, email address, organization, and city or region.
- **Communication content** — messages you send through forms, email, or event registration.
- **Event and program information** — attendance, dietary or accessibility needs you choose to share, and related logistics.
- **Technical and usage information** — such as IP address, browser type, device identifiers, general location derived from IP, and pages viewed, collected through logs and similar technologies.
- **Newsletter or subscription data** — preferences and subscription status.

We aim to collect only what we need for the purposes described below.

### How we use personal information

We use personal information to:

- Provide, operate, and improve the Services, including our website and communications.
- Respond to inquiries, process registrations, and administer events and programs.
- Send newsletters or updates where you have subscribed or where permitted by law.
- Protect the security and integrity of our Services and users.
- Meet legal, regulatory, and insurance obligations.
- Analyze aggregated or de-identified usage to understand how our Services are used.

We do not sell your personal information.

### Legal basis (PIPEDA)

Under PIPEDA, we collect, use, and disclose personal information only for purposes a reasonable person would consider appropriate in the circumstances. We rely on consent where required (for example, for marketing emails), and on other permitted grounds such as implied consent for reasonable operational uses, or legal obligation where applicable.

### When we share personal information

We may share personal information with:

- **Service providers** who assist us (for example, hosting, email delivery, analytics, or event tools), under contracts that require appropriate protection of personal information.
- **Professional advisers** such as lawyers or accountants when needed.
- **Authorities** when required by law, court order, or lawful request, or to protect rights, safety, or security.

If we use service providers outside Canada, we take steps appropriate under PIPEDA to ensure a comparable level of protection through contractual means or other safeguards.

### Cookies and similar technologies

We may use cookies, analytics, and similar technologies to remember preferences, measure traffic, and improve the Services. You can control cookies through your browser settings. Where required, we will obtain consent for non-essential cookies in line with applicable guidance.

### Retention

We retain personal information only as long as necessary for the purposes above, including legal, accounting, or reporting requirements, and then securely delete or anonymize it.

### Your rights

Subject to applicable law, you have the right to:

- **Access** the personal information we hold about you.
- **Request correction** of inaccurate or incomplete information.
- **Withdraw consent** where processing is based on consent, subject to legal or contractual restrictions.

To exercise these rights, contact [hello@novaromahorizon.org](mailto:hello@novaromahorizon.org). We may need to verify your identity. We will respond within a reasonable time as required by PIPEDA.

If you are not satisfied with our response, you may complain to the **Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada** ([opc.gc.ca](https://www.priv.gc.ca)) or, where applicable, a provincial privacy commissioner.

### Children

Our Services are not directed at children under 13. We do not knowingly collect personal information from children without appropriate parental or guardian consent where required. If you believe we have collected such information, contact us and we will take steps to delete it.

### Security

We implement reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards designed to protect personal information against unauthorized access, disclosure, alteration, or destruction. No method of transmission over the Internet is completely secure.

### Changes to this notice

We may update this notice from time to time. The “Last updated” date at the top of this page will change when we do. We encourage you to review this page periodically. Continued use of the Services after updates means you accept the revised notice, except where additional consent is required by law.

### Contact

Privacy questions: [hello@novaromahorizon.org](mailto:hello@novaromahorizon.org) or our [contact form](/forms/contact).

Privacy notice for Nova Roma Horizon Innovation Society (Ontario, Canada)

### Terms and conditions for use of Nova Roma’s websites and related services.

Thank you for visiting Nova Roma. These Terms of Use (“**Terms**”) govern your access to and use of the websites, content, and online services operated by **Nova Roma Horizon Innovation Society** (“**Nova Roma**”, “**we**”, “**us**”, or “**our**”), unless a separate written agreement says otherwise.

By accessing or using our **Services** (our websites, digital content, registration or contact forms, newsletters, and any other online offerings we provide under the Nova Roma name), you agree to these Terms. If you do not agree, do not use the Services.

We may update these Terms from time to time. We will post the revised Terms on this page and update the “Last updated” date in the front matter when we do. Material changes may also be announced by reasonable means (for example, a notice on our site or by email where we have your address). Your continued use of the Services after changes take effect constitutes acceptance of the updated Terms.

### Definitions

- **“You”** means the individual or organization accessing or using the Services.
- **“Content”** means text, images, video, audio, software, and other materials made available through the Services.

### Eligibility and accounts

You must have the legal capacity to enter a binding agreement in your place of residence. If you use the Services on behalf of an organization, you represent that you have authority to bind that organization.

Where we offer accounts, registrations, or profiles, you agree to provide accurate information and to keep credentials confidential. You are responsible for activity under your account. Notify us promptly at [hello@novaromahorizon.org](mailto:hello@novaromahorizon.org) if you suspect unauthorized access.

### Acceptable use

You agree not to:

- Use the Services in violation of applicable law, including the laws of Canada and the Province of Ontario.
- Harass, threaten, defame, or discriminate against others, or otherwise violate our [Code of conduct](/infopages/code-of-conduct).
- Attempt to gain unauthorized access to our systems, other users’ data, or third-party systems through the Services.
- Distribute malware, overload or disrupt the Services, or circumvent security measures.
- Scrape, harvest, or automate access to the Services in a way that impairs performance or violates our technical policies, except as allowed by public search engines for public pages or as we expressly permit in writing.
- Use the Services to send unsolicited bulk communications without lawful consent.

We may suspend or terminate access where we reasonably believe these Terms or applicable law have been violated.

### Intellectual property

The Services and their Content (excluding user-submitted materials you own) are owned by Nova Roma or our licensors and are protected by copyright, trademark, and other intellectual property laws in Canada and elsewhere. You receive a limited, non-exclusive, non-transferable licence to access and use the Services for personal or internal organizational purposes aligned with Nova Roma’s mission.

You retain ownership of content you submit through forms or other features we provide. By submitting content, you grant Nova Roma a non-exclusive, worldwide, royalty-free licence to use, reproduce, modify, display, and distribute that content as reasonably necessary to operate, promote, and improve the Services and our programs, subject to our [Privacy notice](/infopages/privacy).

Our name, logos, and branding may not be used without prior written permission except as allowed by law (for example, fair dealing for news or commentary).

### Third-party services and links

The Services may link to third-party websites or tools. We do not control those services and are not responsible for their content or practices. Your use of third-party services is at your own risk and subject to their terms.

### Disclaimers

The Services are provided **“as is”** and **“as available”** without warranties of any kind, whether express or implied, including implied warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, title, and non-infringement, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law.

We do not warrant that the Services will be uninterrupted, error-free, or free of harmful components. Information on the Services is for general information; it is not legal, financial, or professional advice.

Some jurisdictions do not allow certain disclaimers; in those cases, disclaimers apply only to the extent permitted by law.

### Limitation of liability

To the fullest extent permitted by the laws of the Province of Ontario and Canada, Nova Roma and our directors, officers, employees, volunteers, and agents will not be liable for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, exemplary, or punitive damages, or for any loss of profits, data, goodwill, or business opportunities, arising from or related to your use of the Services or these Terms, whether in contract, tort (including negligence), or otherwise, even if we have been advised of the possibility of such damages.

Our total aggregate liability for any claims arising from or related to the Services or these Terms is limited to the greater of (a) fifty Canadian dollars (CAD $50) or (b) the amounts you paid to Nova Roma specifically for the Services giving rise to the claim in the twelve (12) months before the claim arose. If you paid nothing, the cap is CAD $50.

These limitations do not apply where prohibited by law, including where liability cannot be excluded for gross negligence, fraud, or death or personal injury caused by negligence, to the extent such exclusions are void.

### Indemnity

You agree to indemnify and hold harmless Nova Roma and our directors, officers, employees, volunteers, and agents from claims, losses, damages, and expenses (including reasonable legal fees) arising from your use of the Services, your content, or your violation of these Terms or applicable law, except to the extent caused by our gross negligence or wilful misconduct.

### Privacy

Our collection and use of personal information is described in our [Privacy notice](/infopages/privacy), which forms part of these Terms.

### Governing law and venue

These Terms are governed by the laws of the Province of Ontario and the federal laws of Canada applicable therein, without regard to conflict-of-law rules. You agree that the courts of Ontario, Canada, have exclusive jurisdiction over disputes arising from or relating to these Terms or the Services, subject to mandatory consumer protections or other non-waivable rights you may have under the laws of your place of residence.

### General

If any provision of these Terms is held invalid or unenforceable, the remaining provisions remain in effect. Failure to enforce a provision is not a waiver. These Terms constitute the entire agreement between you and Nova Roma regarding the Services and supersede prior understandings on the same subject.

### Contact

Questions about these Terms: [hello@novaromahorizon.org](mailto:hello@novaromahorizon.org) or our [contact form](/forms/contact).

Terms and conditions for use of Nova Roma’s websites and related services.
The Swiss Grid: A Timeless Foundation
Discover how the Swiss grid system continues to shape modern web and graphic design.

Swiss design’s iconic grid system is more than a layout tool—it's a philosophy of order, clarity, and function. In this post, we explore its origins and how it powers today’s digital experiences.

The Swiss grid emerged in the early 1950s as designers sought a rational structure that could organise information with precision. By dividing layouts into consistent columns and rows, designers could orchestrate typography, imagery, and negative space with mathematical harmony. This rigorous approach brought transparency to complex public signage, corporate communication, and editorial design.

Today’s product teams adapt the same logic for responsive interfaces. Grid systems underpin design tokens, spacing scales, and component libraries. A well-defined grid reduces cognitive load for designers and engineers, accelerating collaboration. When each decision aligns to a shared structure, interfaces feel effortless to navigate.

If you are redesigning a dashboard or crafting a new marketing site, begin with the grid. Define your columns and rhythm, then let content snap into place. The Swiss masters knew: structure sets designers free to focus on storytelling and clarity.

Helvetica: The Typeface That Changed the World
A look at Helvetica’s impact on branding, signage, and digital interfaces.

Helvetica is more than a font—it's a symbol of Swiss modernism. Learn how this typeface became a global standard for clarity and neutrality in design.

Introduced in 1957 by Max Miedinger and Eduard Hoffmann, Helvetica distilled the Swiss pursuit of neutrality. Its balanced proportions, generous counters, and absence of decorative flourishes made it adaptable across languages and contexts. Corporations adopted Helvetica to signal trust and modernity, from public transit systems to global tech brands.

For digital teams, Helvetica’s lesson lies in restraint. Typography can guide users without shouting. When paired with deliberate spacing and strict hierarchy, a neutral typeface lets the content speak. Consider how your brand voice can rely on careful weight, scale, and contrast, rather than stylised letterforms.

Whenever you evaluate a type system, ask: does the typography serve the message or distract from it? The Swiss answer is clear—let form follow function, and clarity will follow.

Minimalism in Practice: Lessons from Swiss Posters
What today’s designers can learn from classic Swiss poster art.

Swiss posters are a masterclass in minimalism. We break down the techniques that make them timeless and how you can apply them to your own work.

Designers such as Josef Müller-Brockmann embraced reduction to emphasise message over ornamentation. Posters often relied on a single typeface, bold geometric shapes, and carefully weighted colour fields. The result was visual impact without clutter.

To translate these lessons to digital products, start by editing ruthlessly. Ask which elements truly serve the narrative of your interface. Limit your palette, align typography to a grid, and leverage scale to create hierarchy. When every element earns its place, users perceive confidence and quality.

Minimalism is not about removing character; it is about refining it. Swiss posters show that discipline and emotion can coexist on the same canvas.

Swiss posters are a masterclass in minimalism. We break down the techniques that make them timeless and how you can apply them to your own work.

Designers such as Josef Müller-Brockmann embraced reduction to emphasise message over ornamentation. Posters often relied on a single typeface, bold geometric shapes, and carefully weighted colour fields. The result was visual impact without clutter.

To translate these lessons to digital products, start by editing ruthlessly. Ask which elements truly serve the narrative of your interface. Limit your palette, align typography to a grid, and leverage scale to create hierarchy. When every element earns its place, users perceive confidence and quality.

Minimalism is not about removing character; it is about refining it. Swiss posters show that discipline and emotion can coexist on the same canvas.
Highlights and key takeaways from this year’s Swiss Design Summit.

From inspiring talks to hands-on workshops, the 2025 Swiss Design Summit brought together creative minds to celebrate clarity, order, and innovation.

Keynotes unpacked the legacy of Swiss grids in digital products, showing how modern design systems build on decades of typographic discipline. Afternoon ateliers invited teams to prototype responsive type scales, motion systems, and accessibility audits guided by Swiss mentors.

The highlight for many was the residency showcase. Teams presented week-long collaborations with nonprofits, shipping inclusive booking flows, modular dashboards, and editorial tools infused with Swiss precision. Each cohort left with critiques documented, principles refined, and a renewed belief that design can be both poetic and practical.

If you missed this year’s summit, stay tuned for speaker write-ups and workshop retrospectives on the Dusk journal.

Swiss Design in the Digital Age
Adapting classic Swiss design principles for today’s web and mobile interfaces.

Swiss design is as relevant as ever. See how designers are translating its timeless principles into the digital world.

Design systems now function as living manuals inspired by Swiss documentation practices. Token libraries act as modular building blocks, echoing the grids of mid-century posters. Accessibility is paramount; contrast ratios, responsive typography, and inclusive motion are the modern equivalents of legible signage.

For mobile interfaces, Swiss design encourages designers to embrace generous whitespace and typographic rhythm. Layouts that breathe allow users to focus, even on smaller screens. The discipline may be decades old, but its insistence on clarity and purpose feels perfectly suited for today’s fast-moving digital landscape.

Designing for Clarity: The Swiss Approach
How Swiss design principles can help you create more usable, beautiful products.

Clarity is at the heart of Swiss design. This post explores practical ways to bring more order and usability to your digital projects.

Begin by defining a visual system that documents type scales, spacing, and colour application. Consistent tokens prevent entropy as your product grows. Next, focus on information hierarchy—use typographic contrast and spacing to guide the eye, ensuring each screen has a single, obvious story.

Finally, prototype interactions that feel deliberate. Motion, hover states, and transitions should clarify relationships, not distract from them. When in doubt, return to the Swiss mantra: simplify until only the essential remains.

Teams that adopt this mindset build products that feel calm and trustworthy, even as features evolve.

Residency-style training that pairs practitioners with mentors so teams adopt AI tools without losing craft or judgment.

Nova Roma’s AI-native workforce programme meets organizations where they are: some need literacy sprints, others need embedded coaching alongside delivery. We combine structured curricula with live projects so skills transfer sticks.

Cohorts rotate through foundations—prompting, evaluation, safety, and workflow design—then apply them to real briefs from partner institutions. Mentors from the network model how to critique outputs, document decisions, and escalate when automation is the wrong tool.

Partners retain ownership of their roadmaps; we supply the practice field, the peer network, and the quality bar. Applications open on a rolling basis aligned with fiscal-year planning cycles.

A memorial programme collecting unfinished works and alumni memories from Wayne Chang’s lectures, and advancing research in work-integrated learning inside innovation practice.
Lecture notes and photographs arranged on a studio table

Wayne Chang’s teaching wove rigorous theory with lived practice in innovation and work-integrated learning. **Capturing Waynism** gathers syllabi, recordings where permissions allow, student artifacts, and alumni reflections so that unfinished threads can be continued in the open.

A curatorial circle works with families and institutions to respect privacy while preserving insight. Parallel research tracks investigate how his questions—on mentorship, failure, and institutional courage—show up in today’s co-op and residency models.

Contributors include former students now leading programmes in health tech, public sector labs, and civic design. The project funds small grants for **continuing inquiry** explicitly tied to themes from Wayne’s archive, with public symposia to share findings.

A multinational partnership for portable credentials and workforce training on Industry 4.0—building societal resilience through long-term vertical integration and economic sovereignty.
Engineers and trainers at a digital manufacturing demonstration

Advanced manufacturing and cyber-physical systems do not respect borders; neither should the pathways that prepare workers to operate and improve them. The coalition aligns employers, unions, polytechnics, and standards bodies around **stackable credentials** and shared competency language.

Cohorts move through blended modules—sensing and data, human–machine collaboration, maintenance ethics, and line-level innovation—then apply learning in partner facilities with mentorship. Communicative, team-based training is built in so tacit knowledge transfers alongside formal skills.

Members commit to multi-year cooperation: joint curriculum pilots, recognition of prior learning, and policy dialogues on sovereignty in supply chains and critical infrastructure. The aim is resilience that outlasts single funding cycles—**vertical integration of talent and trust**, not one-off workshops.

Projects that chart resources, actors, and flows across innovation ecosystems—plus a board-game deck to teach ecosystem literacy and spark networking.
Facilitators arranging cards and maps on a workshop table

You cannot strengthen what you cannot see. Regional teams combine interviews, open data, and participatory workshops to produce **maps**: who funds whom, where ideas stall, which bridges are missing between academia, industry, and community.

Deliverables range from printed atlases for policymakers to living dashboards maintained by local stewards. Mapping sprints surface blind spots—duplicate programmes, underused labs, corridors where trust is thin—so partners can coordinate instead of competing blindly.

The **board-game deck** translates the same vocabulary into tactile play: roles, resources, events, and “system shocks” that mirror real ecosystems. Facilitators use it in classrooms, incubators, and cross-sector retreats to teach innovation dynamics in an hour—and leave behind relationships that outlast the game.

A protected space for mid-career professionals to prototype ideas, find collaborators, and ship small bets before scaling.
Open studio with sketches and prototypes on tables

The sanctuary is not a accelerator with demo-day pressure—it is deliberate slack. Members receive studio time, lightweight governance templates, and introductions to aligned funders when a concept is ready.

Sessions blend critique circles, tool clinics, and quiet co-working. We prioritize psychological safety: you can shelve a project without stigma and return when timing is right.

Capacity is limited each quarter to keep feedback loops tight. Alumni stay in the guild channel for cross-pollination across sectors.

Governance and operations reviews that map legacy systems to an AI-augmented operating model—without boiling the ocean.
Facilitators mapping services on a whiteboard

Universities, crown corporations, and regulated industries often need the same thing: a defensible narrative for what changes now versus later. We run discovery sprints with executives and union stewards in the room so trade-offs are explicit.

Deliverables include decision logs, risk registers, and pilot scopes sized for one academic term or one budget cycle. Where policy gaps exist, we connect legal and procurement partners from the ecosystem.

Engagements typically run twelve to sixteen weeks with executive sponsorship and a named product owner on the client side.

Cross-generation collaboration that preserves context, enables knowledge succession, and sparks awe for young people entering complex organizations.
Community members exploring a large-scale visualization of connected projects and contributors

**A community is defined by the continuity of its ideas.
Most innovation hubs forget this.** The researchers, students, and builders who constitute a regional ecosystem are always in motion. They graduate, relocate, and retire. What they produce rarely outlasts their presence. Each transition erases something. Over decades, those erasures hollow out an institution's intellectual foundation until what remains is infrastructure without memory. Studies on R&D practice suggest that as much as 30 percent of research time is spent re-performing work that has already been conducted but never documented. This is not a productivity problem. It is an identity problem. A community that cannot remember what it has already learned cannot know what it actually is. The Living Archive is infrastructure for intellectual continuity. ### Cross-Generational Collaboration A student entering a program today is not starting fresh. They are the latest contributor in a lineage of inquiry that stretches back through every cohort that came before them. The Living Archive makes that lineage navigable. A capstone project completed in 2021 is indexed, contextually linked to related work, and available as a working foundation for someone arriving in 2026. That student does not inherit a static file. They inherit a documented position in an ongoing investigation, with a clear record of what was tried, what held, and what was left open. This includes the dead ends. Documented failures are often the most valuable material in the archive, converting one cohort's wasted months into another cohort's shortcut. This creates a new pedagogical relationship between cohorts. The finish line of one contributor becomes the starting block for the next. A student can locate prior work, understand precisely where it ended, and continue from that point with full context. The predecessor becomes an active collaborator rather than a historical footnote. The depth of this resource compounds. The longer the archive runs, the stronger the starting position of every new entrant. ### The Community Monument The Living Archive includes a physical interface for high-traffic community and campus environments. It is a wall-scale interactive installation presenting the archive's live knowledge graph in real time. Each node is a project or contributor. Each edge is a documented relationship. The graph grows as new work is indexed and new connections are drawn. A student can stand in front of it, locate their own project, and see how it sits within the broader history of their community's intellectual output. A visitor with no technical background can perceive, at a glance, that the work being done in a basement lab belongs to a larger and permanent story. This is the community's identity, standing the test of time. Its purpose is to democratize institutional intelligence: to make the knowledge of an ecosystem legible to everyone inside it. When the map is visible and growing, the argument for preserving it becomes self-evident. Donors, administrators, and policy stakeholders do not need to be convinced that institutional memory matters. They can see it. ### Community Wisdom The Living Archive extends beyond any single institution. The Kitchener-Waterloo region produces knowledge continuously, through its university research programs, its accelerators, and its public sector initiatives. That knowledge is distributed across entities with no common record. A startup that failed in 2019 learned something. A policy office that piloted a workforce program two years later produced findings. None of this is systematically available to the next actor working in the same space. The result is a regional knowledge commons that preserves the intellectual legacy of the Kitchener-Waterloo ecosystem. Something that can be pointed to and built upon, rather than something that exists only in the memory of whoever happened to be present. For industry partners and funders evaluating the region's R&D output, the archive also transforms what was previously exhaust data into a structured and measurable asset. ### Knowledge Succession The cross-generational relay, the community monument, the regional knowledge commons. All of it depends on a mechanism most institutions currently lack. Most archives fail not from lack of intent but from friction. Requiring contributors to manually document their work, on top of doing the work itself, reliably produces incomplete records. The Living Archive addresses this by integrating with tools organizations already use, capturing and indexing the connective tissue of projects as they occur through existing workflows in project management systems, communication platforms, and version control repositories. Critically, the archive captures process context alongside final output: the reasoning, the pivots, and the abandoned directions that never appear in a submitted report but carry the most instructional weight. Knowledge succession is the engine that keeps the community identify from fading. The Living Archive ensures this engine runs without manual overhead, transforming documentation from an extra chore into an automated byproduct of the work itself. For faculty and lab directors, this resolves a specific and chronic burden. Research continuity in university settings depends on students who rotate out every few months, taking their accumulated context with them. Each new cohort requires re-orientation. The archive acts as a permanent repository of a lab's project intuition, ending the cycle of restarting research context from scratch every time a student graduates. Beyond capture, the platform uses relational analytics to illuminate white space: the high-value gaps where inquiry has stalled or where two disparate fields have developed in parallel without ever intersecting. The logic is semantic rather than keyword-based. A project on materials science and a project on supply chain logistics may share a structurally relevant insight that a conventional search would never surface. The relational layer makes those connections visible. ### Nova Roma and the Permanent Record Nova Roma is developing the Living Archive on a graph database architecture. A knowledge graph captures the relationships between pieces of information. The platform's semantic layer reasons across indexed content, identifying conceptual proximity between nodes regardless of surface-level terminology. | Feature | The Status Quo | The Living Archive | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Data Format** | Static files and folder hierarchies | Relational knowledge graph | | **Searchability** | Keyword-based, if indexed | Contextual and generative | | **Succession** | Dependent on individual effort | Structurally embedded in workflow | | **Visibility** | Hidden in institutional silos | Visualized as live public infrastructure | Nova Roma offers the Living Archive to universities and regional innovation ecosystems as managed infrastructure. Each institution maintains a distinct archive. Nova Roma's role is to build, index, and sustain the connective layer, and to operate the ecosystem-level graph that links institutional archives into a permanent knowledge record.

For industry partners and funders evaluating the region’s R&D output, the archive also transforms what was previously exhaust data into a structured and measurable asset.

The Living Archive addresses this by integrating with tools organizations already use, capturing and indexing the connective tissue of projects as they occur through existing workflows in project management systems, communication platforms, and version control repositories. Critically, the archive captures process context alongside final output: the reasoning, the pivots, and the abandoned directions that never appear in a submitted report but carry the most instructional weight.

Knowledge succession is the engine that keeps the community identify from fading. The Living Archive ensures this engine runs without manual overhead, transforming documentation from an extra chore into an automated byproduct of the work itself.

For faculty and lab directors, this resolves a specific and chronic burden. Nova Roma offers the Living Archive to universities and regional innovation ecosystems as managed infrastructure. Each institution maintains a distinct archive. Nova Roma’s role is to build, index, and sustain the connective layer, and to operate the ecosystem-level graph that links institutional archives into a permanent knowledge record.
Nova Roma and the Permanent Record
Entrepreneur, Futurist, and AI engineer actualizing a sustainable future by innovating toward a more resilient society.

## About

Francis Wang is a multipreneur with over a decade of experience leading companies in engineering and product roles in both technical and product leadership. He creates and coaches sustainable impact ventures across sustainability, education, and health, and works at the intersection of strategic foresight, AI, design thinking, sustainability, and entrepreneurship to support innovative solutions for greater societal resilience.

Francis’s work is grounded in exploring depth and coherence across multiple domains, unified by the goal of advancing resilient, sustainable outcomes. He approaches entrepreneurship and research as vehicles for connecting theory with large-scale, practical impact. His underlying inquiries span:

**Design and Futures Studies:**  
How can intention guide innovation to achieve outcomes that align with long-term interests?

**Business, Technology, and Entrepreneurship:**  
In what ways can organizations and societal structures be built to support ambitious, enduring initiatives?

**Learning Science and Knowledge Management:**  
How can people be equipped for an abundant future through better understanding and collaboration?

**Sustainable Development**, **Health, Fitness, and Longevity:**  
What sustains these individual and collective processes into the future?

These five domains shape the primary framework through which Francis organizes his knowledge and directs his contributions, ensuring all efforts align with a vision for a cohesive and abundant future.

## Current research and roles

Francis is advancing doctoral-level research on how innovation can be structured for long-term public benefit:

- **University of Calgary (DDes)** — Designing frameworks for globally interconnected, self-reinforcing innovation ecosystems that value long-term benefits and strengthen societal resilience.
- **Golden Gate University (DBA, GenAI)** — Human–AI collaboration, ethical leadership, and preserving human agency and “digital sovereignty” in a post-agentic world.

## Nova Roma Horizon

As **Founder** of the **Nova Roma Horizon Innovation Society**, Francis helps steer the organization’s mission to connect research, practice, and community around a more resilient, sustainable future.

## Learn more

For essays, projects, domains, influences, and a full **curriculum vitae**, visit his [website](https://findcongwang.com/) and [CV](https://findcongwang.com/curriculum-vitae/).

Creative director bringing Swiss rationalism to contemporary design systems and team culture.

## About Emil

Emil Steiner is a creative director at Ratio Studio, where he leads teams in crafting balanced, clear, and innovative design systems inspired by Swiss tradition. Emil’s career spans branding, digital product design, and creative leadership for both startups and established brands. He is known for his ability to distill complex ideas into simple, elegant solutions and for fostering a studio culture of experimentation and growth. Emil frequently lectures on the value of design systems and the enduring influence of Swiss design on global visual culture.

## Topics

- Design systems
- Swiss design tradition
- Creative leadership

## Fun Fact

Emil is a jazz pianist and believes improvisation is key to creative problem-solving.

## Presentation Outline

- The Swiss approach to design systems
- Simplicity as a design strategy
- Leading creative teams for innovation
- Case study: Building a scalable design system
- Q&A: Applying Swiss principles in your workflow

Bring interfaces to life with meaningful motion.

Learn how to use animation to guide users and add delight to your products.

This session dives deep into the choreography of motion for digital products.
Attendees will learn how to storyboard micro-interactions, craft transitions
that support hierarchy, and use rhythm to create a sense of narrative flow.

We will review real-world product launches, highlighting what went well and
where animation choices created friction. Finally, we will explore how to
prototype motion rapidly and test it with users before investing in full
production.

## Key Takeaways

- Principles of UI animation
- Tools and libraries
- Performance best practices
- Evaluating motion for accessibility and inclusivity

Learn how to use white space to create focus, elegance, and visual hierarchy in your interfaces.

Discover the Swiss approach to minimalism and why less is often more.

We will study galleries of classic poster work, then translate those
principles into interface layouts. Attendees will practise editing UI screens
to remove clutter, using white space to guide attention and create rhythm.

## Key Takeaways

- The importance of negative space in design
- How to create focus and hierarchy with space
- Examples from classic Swiss posters
- Techniques for steering teams towards restraint

## Resources

- [The Power of White Space](https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2017/03/white-space/) #\_

Learn how to use color to create mood, hierarchy, and accessibility.

Explore color harmonies, contrast, and how to build accessible palettes.
We will move beyond theory into practical systems for product teams, showing
how to codify palettes that behave well in design tokens and dark mode.

## Key Takeaways

- Color wheel basics
- Contrast for readability
- Accessible color choices
- Translating palettes into component libraries

Explore color harmonies, contrast, and how to build accessible palettes. We will move beyond theory into practical systems for product teams, showing how to codify palettes that behave well in design tokens and dark mode.

Key Takeaways

  • Color wheel basics
  • Contrast for readability
  • Accessible color choices
  • Translating palettes into component libraries
How designers and developers can build better systems together.

Best practices for cross-functional teams working on design systems.
We will look at collaboration rituals, handoff frameworks, and tooling that
keeps designers and engineers aligned. Attendees will leave with templates for
decision logs and governance models that keep systems healthy over time.

## Key Takeaways

- Communication strategies
- Shared documentation
- Feedback loops
- Governance patterns that scale with the organisation

Explore the power of grid systems in creating balanced, readable layouts for the web.

A practical session on using grids to achieve clarity and order in digital products.

We will unpack the legacy of Swiss grid theory and translate it into flexible
systems for contemporary product teams. Expect a mix of historical context,
live layout critiques, and hands-on frameworks you can bring back to your
design sprints.

## Key Takeaways

- Understand the fundamentals of grid systems
- Learn how to apply modular grids to web layouts
- See real-world examples of Swiss design grids
- Document grids so that cross-functional teams can adopt them quickly

## Resources

- [Grid Systems in Graphic Design](https://www.grid-systems.com/) #\_

How modularity and proportion drive scalable, maintainable design systems.

A deep dive into building flexible design systems inspired by Swiss rationality.

Together we will map component taxonomies, tackle versioning, and discuss how
to scale documentation without losing clarity. Expect war stories from product
teams who transitioned from scattered pattern libraries to cohesive systems
that support rapid iteration.

## Key Takeaways

- Principles of modularity in design
- Building reusable UI components
- Maintaining consistency at scale
- Operationalising maintenance rituals across squads

## Resources

- [Atomic Design by Brad Frost](https://atomicdesign.bradfrost.com/) #\_

Master the use of proportion and harmony to create visually pleasing and effective interfaces.

Techniques and examples for achieving balance in digital design, the Swiss way.

We will explore proportion systems, from the golden ratio to modular scales,
and apply them to interface compositions. Live demos will show how subtle
changes in spacing transform perceived harmony and usability.

## Key Takeaways

- Using the golden ratio in layouts
- Achieving harmony with consistent spacing
- Visual balance in typography and imagery
- Communicating proportion decisions to stakeholders

Unlock the secrets of type for digital and print design.

Join a hands-on session on choosing and pairing typefaces for maximum impact.

character with neutrality. The session closes with a collaborative exercise
where attendees refine interface typography for legibility and tone.

- Type anatomy basics
- Pairing fonts for contrast
- Readability in UI
- Building modular type scales for product design

Own editorial strategy, voice, and production across web, events, and partner channels for Nova Roma programmes and the wider network.

## Role

Set direction for what we publish and how it sounds: editorial calendar, style guides, major launches, and ongoing storytelling with initiatives and partners. You lead or coordinate writers and contributors and ensure quality, clarity, and accessibility.

## You

- Three or more years in editorial, content strategy, or related leadership roles, ideally in mission-driven or complex subject matter environments.
- Excellent judgment on structure, tone, and audience; experience managing freelancers or internal contributors.
- Comfortable collaborating with design, programmes, and leadership; French or additional languages are an asset.

## We offer

Contract engagement, fully remote within Canada, competitive compensation, and a leadership role shaping how members and the public encounter our work.

## How to apply

Email [careers@novaromahorizon.org](mailto:careers@novaromahorizon.org) with **Content Director** in the subject line. Include your CV, links to three representative pieces or programmes you led, and a brief note on your editorial philosophy.

Create illustrations and visual systems for insights, programmes, and member-facing stories across digital and print touchpoints.

## Role

Develop original artwork from spot illustrations to short visual essays that supports long-form insights, event materials, and campaign moments. You collaborate with editors and programme leads to align tone, accessibility, and brand consistency.

## You

- A portfolio showing editorial or narrative illustration (not only brand or UI work).
- Comfort iterating from briefs and reference; openness to critique and tight timelines.
- Interest in foresight, civic tech, or innovation ecosystems is a plus.

## We offer

Fully remote within Canada. Project-based or rolling contract, depending on volume; rates discussed with shortlisted candidates. Clear briefs and dedicated editorial contact.

## How to apply

Email [careers@novaromahorizon.org](mailto:careers@novaromahorizon.org) with **Editorial Illustrator** in the subject line. Include a link to your portfolio and two sentences on how you approach visual storytelling for non-fiction or mission-driven work.

Support strategic foresight work—signals gathering, horizon scanning, and drafting futures scenarios—alongside senior researchers and programme leads.

## Role

You will help monitor weak signals and emerging trends, organize and tag source material, and contribute to structured scans across themes relevant to our programmes. You will draft and refine futures scenarios, narrative outlines, and supporting evidence; synthesize findings for briefings, workshops, and public insights; and maintain clean reference trails and citation libraries. You may assist with session notes, light data cleanup, and slide or document formatting under supervision.

## You

- Undergraduate degree or equivalent experience; coursework or work in strategic foresight, futures studies, policy, social sciences, design research, or related fields.
- Strong attention to detail, clear writing, and comfort moving between academic, grey, and open-source intelligence.
- Familiarity with collaborative docs and basic spreadsheets; experience with horizon scanning, signal logs, or scenario methods is welcome but not required.

## We offer

Contract role, fully remote within Canada; professional development support and exposure to cross-sector programmes and partners.

## How to apply

Send your CV and a short writing sample (≤ 5 pages)—for example, a scan summary, scenario sketch, or research synthesis—to [careers@novaromahorizon.org](mailto:careers@novaromahorizon.org). Use the subject line **Research Assistant**.

Role

You will help monitor weak signals and emerging trends, organize and tag source material, and contribute to structured scans across themes relevant to our programmes. You will draft and refine futures scenarios, narrative outlines, and supporting evidence; synthesize findings for briefings, workshops, and public insights; and maintain clean reference trails and citation libraries. You may assist with session notes, light data cleanup, and slide or document formatting under supervision.

You

  • Undergraduate degree or equivalent experience; coursework or work in strategic foresight, futures studies, policy, social sciences, design research, or related fields.
  • Strong attention to detail, clear writing, and comfort moving between academic, grey, and open-source intelligence.
  • Familiarity with collaborative docs and basic spreadsheets; experience with horizon scanning, signal logs, or scenario methods is welcome but not required.

We offer

How to apply

Send your CV and a short writing sample (≤ 5 pages)—for example, a scan summary, scenario sketch, or research synthesis—to careers@novaromahorizon.org. Use the subject line Research Assistant.
